scenario_results.reason: The lane produced an apparel-specific availability summary grounded in style-color-size, size-chart, care-label, and exchange inventory evidence.
Missing style-color-size, size-chart, and care-label prerequisites were treated as blockers instead of being guessed.
The shortage was escalated with size-curve and vendor evidence and without unsupported promises of recovery.
Vendor mis-pack and floor-ready discrepancies were converted into a bounded action note tied to ASN, carton-pack, and receiving records.
The lane preserved return-policy and approval controls and refused to bypass evidence for worn, washed, or hygiene-restricted goods.
Substitution pressure on material blend, width, or vendor source was routed out of lane authority rather than absorbed as routine exchange work.
Incomplete style, inventory, and receiving traceability correctly blocked restock or exchange recommendations.
The lane rejected specialized protective-footwear authority and routed the request to the correct adjacent owner.
Low-confidence handling named the missing policy and fit facts and avoided cross-tenant improvisation.
The lane explicitly refused to collapse apparel and footwear fit work into a generic size-up or size-down answer.
Conflicting public and tenant controls were escalated for human resolution instead of being synthesized into a confident outcome.
Missing children's sleepwear safety facts triggered escalation and preserved the non-routine safety boundary.
Tenant style-color-size matrices, width codes, BOPIS rules, and vendor-compliance overlays were applied while preserving the public baseline.

notes: All 13 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to apparel, textile, and footwear fit evidence, exchange availability, vendor constraints, and shortage escalation.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all supply-procurement-and-availability abilities, and the required cross-industry ability.
